task: Check patrons' identification to ensure that they meet minimum age requirements for consumption of alcoholic beverages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking a single patron's ID. A competent human takes ~20–30 seconds to examine, read, and verify an ID card.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/authenticating varied ID documents (glare, holograms, worn cards), judging authenticity, and social interaction. Vision-based OCR on IDs is feasible, but hologram/forgery detection, varied lighting, and confident autonomous social enforcement remain very challenging for current humanoids.

task: Punch holes in slate, tile, terra cotta, or wooden shingles, using punches and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = punching one hole in a single shingle. A skilled roofer positions the punch and strikes with a hammer in seconds; ~1 minute accounts for positioning and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise two-hand coordination (hold punch, swing hammer), controlled impact force on brittle materials (slate, terra cotta crack easily), and typically done on a sloped roof surface — poor mobility, dexterity, and force-control for current humanoids.

task: Tend assembly lines, performing a few of the many cuts needed to process a carcass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = performing one set of assigned cuts on a single carcass passing on the line; skilled meat-processing workers complete their specific cuts in under a minute per carcass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, force-controlled cuts on irregular, deformable biological material at speed; current humanoids lack the fine dexterous tool control, food-safe compliance, and real-time adaptation to variable carcass geometry needed for acceptable yield and safety.

task: Place blocks under rebar to hold the bars off the deck when reinforcing floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single block (chair/spacer) under one rebar bar. A worker positions and sets one block in ~1–2 minutes across a floor pour.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor/rough concrete deck terrain is challenging for locomotion. Grasping small plastic/concrete chairs and placing them precisely under rebar in cluttered, uneven job-site conditions strains current dexterity and generalization. Heavy debris and safety hazards further limit autonomy.

task: Assist passengers in placing carry-on luggage in overhead, garment, or under-seat storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger with their carry-on. A flight attendant typically lifts or repositions one bag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a very narrow, crowded aircraft aisle, handling bags of highly varied size/weight/shape, lifting overhead in confined space, avoiding passengers, and socially coordinating — all far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured real environments.

task: Inspect soiled articles to determine sources of stains, to locate color imperfections, and to identify items requiring special treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single garment or article. A skilled laundry/dry-cleaning worker visually examines one item for stains, color flaws, and special treatment needs in roughly 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual perception to classify stain types, subtle color imperfections, and fabric conditions. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained textile inspection, stain-source reasoning, and expert-level material identification needed for acceptable quality.

task: Weigh packages or letters to determine postage needed, using weighing scales and rate charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single package/letter, reading the scale, and looking up the rate chart to determine postage. A competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can pick up and place items on a scale and read digital displays via vision, but interpreting rate charts, handling varied package shapes/sizes, and confirming correct postage requires reliable perception, manipulation, and document reading — still error-prone for frontier humanoids.

task: Check victims for signs of life, such as breathing and pulse.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking one victim. A competent responder takes ~1–3 minutes to assess breathing (look/listen/feel) and pulse (carotid/radial check).
robot_success_rationale: Requires subtle tactile sensing (pulse detection), nuanced visual/auditory perception of faint breathing, and safe physical contact on a human body in unstructured emergency settings — all well beyond current humanoid reliability. No deployed system handles this robustly.

task: Turn valves to control air or water output of compressors or pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning one valve to set desired output. A competent worker locates, grips, and adjusts a valve in about 1–3 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Valve turning requires precise grip, torque application, and rotational control on varied handle types (wheel, lever, ball). Current humanoids struggle with tool-like force control, irregular handle geometries, and confirming correct output level.

task: Activate machine start-up switches to grind, lap, hone, debar, shear, or cut workpieces, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting up a grinding/cutting machine for a single workpiece run: locate switches, verify settings, activate in sequence. Typically takes a few minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons but struggle with identifying correct switches on varied industrial panels, reading specifications, and verifying machine state. Industrial panel diversity, fine motor precision on toggles, and safety-critical confirmation steps limit success significantly.

task: Add chemicals or materials to workpieces or machines to facilitate bonding or to cool workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying adhesive/flux/coolant to a single workpiece or machine. A skilled worker typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on method (brush, spray, pour).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/chemical dispensing, tool-use (brush, nozzle, bottle), and surface targeting. Current humanoids struggle with fluid handling, variable containers, and ensuring correct coverage/quantity without spilling.

task: Position material or articles in clamps, templates, or hoop frames prior to automatic operation of machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single workpiece into a clamp, template, or hoop frame. A skilled operator typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on alignment precision required.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, grasping flexible/varied materials, and securing into fixtures. Current humanoids handle structured pick-and-place well but struggle with accurate positioning into tight clamps or hoop frames, especially with deformable materials like fabric.

task: Unclamp and remove finished workpieces from tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unclamping and removing a single finished workpiece from a table. A competent worker locates clamps, releases them, and lifts/moves the part in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can reach and grasp objects on flat surfaces, but varied clamp types (toggle, screw, pneumatic) demand fine dexterity and tool-use generalization. Workpiece weight/shape variability further reduces reliability. Feasible in structured settings but inconsistent.

task: Insert heated metal forms into ties and touch up rough places with hand irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing/shaping one necktie with heated forms and hand irons. A skilled presser completes this in ~2–4 minutes per tie.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to insert heated metal forms into a delicate fabric tie, precise pressure/angle control with hand irons, and heat-sensitive touch-up — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and tactile feedback capabilities.

task: Examine gems during processing to ensure accuracy of angles and positions of cuts or bores, using magnifying glasses, loupes, or shadowgraphs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single gem during processing. A skilled gemologist spends ~2–4 minutes examining one stone's cut angles and bore positions under magnification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterous handling of tiny, reflective gems, precise optical tool use (loupe/shadowgraph), and expert visual interpretation of sub-millimeter cut angles. Current robots lack the micro-dexterity, specialized optics integration, and gemological perceptual judgment needed.

task: Place sensitized paper in frames of projection printers, photostats, or other reproduction machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one sheet of sensitized paper into a frame/machine. A skilled operator aligns and secures a sheet in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, delicate sheet handling (thin, light-sensitive paper), accurate alignment in a frame, and operating specialized reproduction equipment. Poor generalization to varied machine types; high risk of misalignment or paper damage.

task: Lock doors and gates of entrances and exits to secure buildings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locking a single door or gate. A competent worker walks to the entrance, checks the door/gate, and locks it (key, deadbolt, padlock, or latch) in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to doors and grasp handles at ~50–70%, but operating keys, deadbolts, padlocks, and varied latch mechanisms requires fine dexterity and generalization that current humanoids struggle with. Outdoor/uneven terrain near gates adds locomotion difficulty.

task: Serve food or beverages to patrons, and prepare or serve specialty dishes at tables as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a single patron or table their ordered food/beverages, including carrying plates/drinks from kitchen/bar and placing them. Typically 2–4 minutes per table visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic navigation in crowded, unpredictable restaurant environments, stable tray/plate carrying, social interaction, and specialty dish preparation tableside — far beyond current humanoid reliability in unstructured public settings.

task: Distribute food to servers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating and handing off one table's order to waiting servers at a kitchen pass. A competent expediter takes ~2–4 minutes per table's worth of dishes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct dishes, reading tickets, dynamic coordination with multiple humans, carrying loaded plates without spilling, and communicating verbally — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterity in a busy kitchen environment.

task: Raise levers to couple and uncouple cars for makeup and breakup of trains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coupling or uncoupling one pair of rail cars. A skilled car-knocker typically takes 2–4 minutes including walking to position and verifying the connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven, outdoor rail yard terrain, locating and grasping a heavy coupler lever under varying orientations/conditions, and timing with moving cars — far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Examine merchandise to ensure that it is correctly priced and displayed and that it functions as advertised.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single merchandise item for correct price tag, display placement, and functional check. A competent retail worker takes ~2–4 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading price labels (OCR feasible), verifying display placement (vision feasible), AND functional testing of the product (highly variable, unpredictable interactions). Generalization across product types is very poor; manipulation and product-specific testing push success low.

task: Make photocopies of correspondence, documents, and other printed matter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying a single document (1–5 pages): place on glass or feed tray, select settings, retrieve copies. Typically 2–4 minutes for a competent office worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine paper handling, precise placement on copier glass, navigating copier UI/buttons, and retrieving output without jamming or misalignment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous paper manipulation and copier UI interaction.

task: Control hydraulic tractors equipped with tree clamps and booms to lift, swing, and bunch sheared trees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bunching a single group of sheared trees (grab, swing, deposit). A skilled feller-buncher operator cycles through this in ~2–4 minutes per bunch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated operation of a large hydraulic tractor with specialized controls, outdoor rough terrain, complex real-time judgment. No humanoid robot can operate heavy forestry machinery autonomously at this level in 2026.

task: Fill hoppers and position spouts to direct flow of flux or manually brush flux onto seams of workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fluxing a single workpiece seam. Filling a hopper/positioning a spout or brushing flux onto a seam takes a skilled worker roughly 3–5 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise seam identification via perception, dexterous brush control or spout alignment on irregular weld seams, and handling viscous flux material. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and seam-tracing accuracy for industrial quality standards.

task: Assemble meal trays with foods in accordance with patients' diets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one patient meal tray. A trained dietary aide typically takes 3–5 minutes to read the diet order, select correct foods/portions, and arrange them on a tray.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diet orders, identifying correct food items, portioning, and precise placement—combining language understanding, fine dexterous manipulation of varied soft/liquid foods, and dietary compliance logic. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in an unstructured kitchen/cafeteria setting.
